speaker: interviewer
text: This is the beginning of tape number four on July 20-- August 29, 1996. The interview of Jack Arnel. Mr. Arnel, we left off where you were explaining how your mother made contact with Father David.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Father David was looking for us. And at a given moment, we had a door that was leading to the outside-- a secret door, sort of. It was boarded up sort of, but it was made so that we can open up. And we opened it up. And he snuck into. And we had a reunion with Father David. And Father David, he brought us a little food as well at that time. And he said he was looking for us for a long time. Many times, he took walks there. He couldn't make any contact with us. And his mission was that he saw that whatever-- whatever the future holds in store, he doesn't know, but he's willing to risk it and take me away to-- to his church, because there were a lot of young-- Russian youngsters who were being taught the priesthoods over there. And he would hide me amongst them. And when the war ends, you know, and we-- they survived, he would come and-- and-- and take me away. Now, that was a very, very genuine effort on his part. And my mother said to him that we're gonna think about it. We're gonna come to a conclusion, and that next time he'll pass by, we'll let him know. And he left. He was-- also had tears in his eyes. And my father-- [INAUDIBLE] my father and my mother discussing this matter. And they-- they decided not to do it, because they were afraid that the children that I would be exposed to-- the rest of them may expose me, and I'll be caught and I'll be killed right on the spot by the Nazis. So that's why I did not go into the church at the time. And that was a very valiant effort on behalf of Father David. There was another gentleman, a Pole, who was in the fur business, who my father did work-- some contract work before the war, who made contact with us in the same manner, who needed certain work to be done on behalf of his Polish clientele. And he used to also come by, and sneak in, and bring some of the fur skins for my father to do the work. And he would pay us with food. And this was a result that for quite some time, we were able to get more food to-- to reduce the hunger. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't think of it right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So we-- we left off that you were in Kailis. And they've taken the children away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you actually stay in Kailis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, in Kailis-- we moved to Kailis from the ghetto in-- let's see. Was it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you went into the ghetto in September 1941, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: In September-- in October, that's right. In October of 1941, we moved to Kailis. And there-- we remained there up until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] April of 1944. It was a period of more than two years. Two-- two and a half years or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the Vilna Ghetto continuing the entire time that you were in Kailis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. The Vilna Ghetto was liquidated in June of 1943. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Most of the people of the Vilna Ghetto were taken to Paneriai little at a time-- 1,000 here, 2,000 there. The Germans made these demands on the people to provide people for labor camps. But we found out later they were taken to Paneriai and killed there in the same method that I described before. And in-- in June of 1943, there were nobody no more-- nobody left [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn about this in Kailis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was some people who escaped from the ghetto. Some people were trying to get to the woods where the partisans were hiding out. There was some Russian partisans. There was some Polish partisans. And at one point, 24 of young Jewish men escaped. And they were the-- actually, the underground. They had some pistols on them, and some weapons. They were supposedly in the ghetto to-- they were going to fight to the bitter end. But somehow, they lost a lot of men in other actions and all that. And so they were trying to reach the woods. But they couldn't make it in one time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came to Kailis. And they snuck in at night. And they told us the entire story about what happened to the remainder of the Jews in the ghetto, how they were taken away. And they knew that at that point, that they would be taken to Paneriai and-- and killed over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we remained in Kailis with a knowledge that the Vilna Ghetto is no more, that all the Jews were killed and slaughtered. And we just didn't know what tomorrow brings to us. We knew that the Germans needed our-- the production of the furs very desperately. They were fighting in the-- at Stalingrad and Leningrad, and the winters were very, very severe, cold in the-- so they maintained this factory. And they maintained the furriers over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually visit the factory yourself? Were you inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went there. There were several occasions that I was able to see the factory. It was a very nice factory. It was the same factory that actually, the Russians had put together with all the equipment from all the furriers that they took away. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Including your father's equipment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Including my father's. As a matter of fact, when I was visiting there on one--- one-- one occasion, my father pointed out his equipment that was sitting over there in the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people worked in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I would say Jewish people, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: All together.

speaker: interviewee
text: All together, there may have been, at that time, maybe 600. There were a lot of Polish people working there too, in the offices and-- and all that. But the main trust of the fur-- furs were Jews, you know? They were the-- the main craftsmen over there. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] up until-- I believe it was-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] there was a time when the front line has changed, that the Russians were now attacking the Germans, and they were throwing the Germans back. And so the front line has come closer towards Vilna. And the-- the Nazis were retreating. The Germans were retreating. And at one point-- I think it was in April of 1944-- that's when they liquidated Kailis. However, before that time-- no, they liquidated it at-- I think at-- October. But in April of 1944, the Germans wanted about a dozen or 15 furriers with their families to be transferred to Kaunas Ghetto-- Kaunas, Lithuania. We didn't know why. We didn't know why-- why-- why they wanted it. However, the selection had to be made amongst the furriers themselves. And my father, he was selected as one of the families that are going to go to Kaunas. And so the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who selected him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amongst the furriers. They had a committee.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: They formed a committee. And they had to select. And he was-- he was selected as one the 12 or 15 furriers, with their families, to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he on the committee, your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was not on the committee. But the committee has selected him. They interviewed some people, and they wanted to know how they feel. My father was willing to go. And I don't know exactly why, but he was willing to go to Kaunas. And as it turns out, they picked us up on a truck and took us to the railroad station in Vilna. And they put us on the train. And that train was going past Paneriai, the graveyard. And we were all trembling at that moment, thinking that perhaps the train is going to stop, and they're gonna take us off and shoot us like the rest of them. But we didn't. A few minutes, it passed the Paneriai-- the Paneriai station and was rolling on. And it did bring us to the Kaunas Ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother or your father and your sister with you on that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was the furriers and their immediate families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to Kailis for-- for a second, you say there were mostly Jews working as furriers, and there were also Poles and Lithuanians--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --working there. They were paid, I guess, for their-- for their work, the Poles? That was like a job for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the Poles, it was a regular job for them, right. The-- the Jews were also paid, but at a very minimum-- like, maybe a quarter of the-- the amount that they were paid, because they were also supplying the food for the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the Jews paid? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Did they receive money, actually? Or were they only--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think-- I think rubles. I-- I don't remember exactly how. I was young at that time. It-- I can't recall how they were paid. But it wasn't much, whatever they paid. They-- it was-- don't forget, we were living in a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were supplying all the food and all the-- I don't even know whether they paid them any money at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the-- was the factory--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was big.

speaker: interviewer
text: --if you can tell us--

speaker: interviewee
text: It--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in, you know-- maybe in meters how wide, how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a large factory. It was originally-- the building was a-- an electric factory. And it was converted to the furs. And I would say it may have been, like, a block long. [INAUDIBLE] a block long.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was the Russians who made the original conversion?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians were the original builders of this factory with all the equipment that was taken away from the furriers, yes. And so they-- they took us to Kaunas. In Kaunas, we thought that perhaps the furriers would be put back to work with furs. But it turned out that they didn't need them anymore there. Somehow the Kaunas Ghetto was also-- they knew that-- they had a knowledge of the liquidation of the Vilna Ghetto. And they lasted until 1944. And we were there in the Kaunas Ghetto about two months or so. And they started liquidating the Kaunas Ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever learn why they picked 15 families to take them to Kaunas?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never, never learned why they did that. It was-- they probably wanted-- they had plans for Kailis. And they wanted to have a remnant of furriers just so that they would have something, because after we were taken away, Kailis was liquidated, including the other small camp, HKP. All the-- for the most [INAUDIBLE], except for those who were able to hide out or escape, were taken to Paneriai, and all were killed. After being incarcerated there for two and a half years, the finale was that they were all killed. Most of them were killed in Paneriai.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, or did you ever know, the name of the German commander of Kailis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was the one in charge-- see, I was so young, I couldn't remember the names. But they referred to him like [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] meaning a-- a bear neck, because in the winter, he had a big, long neck. And so they gave him a nickname. They called him the [NON-ENGLISH]. And that was the man who was in charge of the-- of the facility to-- the security of the camp. Now, the head of the factories, they were civilian people. There was an incident in the factory once where there was a man by the name of Oscar Gluck. He was the one who was in charge, really, of the factory. And there was a-- an-- a story what happened with him. He came to Vilna sometimes during the-- the war. And after the Germans entered Vilna, he met-- he came from Vienna, Austria. He was an Austrian person from Austria. And when the Germans entered, he found one of the German soldiers that was there was a friend of his that he grew up with from Vienna, Austria. And they got to talking to each other. And that Oscar Gluck was actually a Jew. But his friend convinced him that he can get him the job. And he was put in charge of the Kailis factory. And he was given false documentation that he was a Volksdeutsch-- in other words, that he's a German, that he came out of Austria. And he was living under the disguise of a German, a real German. He was-- he actually came from Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at one point, there was a fire in the factory. And there was some investigation by the Gestapo. And during the investigation, as they were investigating, they discovered that this Oscar Gluck is a Jew. And so they arrested him and his wife, and they shot them to death immediately on the spot. So he was actually the head of the Kailis factory for some time. And was there a committee of Jews in Kailis also working in the factory or helping to run the factory, like the committee that picked the 15 people to go to Kaunas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, those were basically-- the committee was to regulate and do certain things to help with our private-- with the lives in the camp. They didn't have much to say in the factory, although they were managers. My father was a manager for a certain section. And the managers were consulted as to production schedules and all that. And he gave advice to the-- to the Germans what we'd do to increase, if necessary if they needed, and how to obtain the-- the skins and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the names of any of the committee members?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't remember them now. They have been documented elsewhere, you know what I mean? But I find I-- I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and back when they took the children away, they saved you because they thought you could work. Did they actually give you a job in Kailis when you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Never?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They-- they thought that I was strong enough to do work. But I did some menial jobs there, sweeping the steps and-- and things like that, shoveling the snow during the winter, or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your mother and the other women? Did they work at Kailis?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They-- for the most part, they were just living in the-- in the house, in the apartments.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you share your apartment in Kailis with-- with another family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, there-- there were certain quarters that they allotted to-- to each family. And it's not that you have an apartment all to yourself. You had to share it with other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you share with there? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: There were other people sharing, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there's one more incident that I want to bring up, what happened in Kailis. And that is the Paneriai-- when the front line got in a little closer in Paneriai, the Germans wanted to-- to hide all the evidence of the-- of the graves over there, of the mass graves with all the people. And so they have taken 70 people out of the ghetto at a time yet to work, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and uncover the graves, and take all these corpses, pile them up, and burn it. They wanted to hide it. And these people were kept in a special place underground, like-- and they were watched very carefully while they were doing this digging and the burning. And they knew, these 70 men, that they are doomed, that they are going to die. And so they started to dig a tunnel with little spoon or a little utensil that they had. And they kept digging a tunnel from that hole that they were held in. And they were successful to dig a 35, 40-foot tunnel. And that tunnel ended right outside of the fence. And at one point, they decided to-- to run away from there. And so they were able to go through the tunnel. And 40 of them made it to the outside, at which point, the German guard noticed it and a mass hunt for them was-- was ensued. And they killed 25 right then and there. And then 15 escaped. 11 made it to the partisans in the woods. But what happened was after that, they needed more people. So they came to Kailis. And they selected 70 men from Kailis. And they took them there to continue this horrendous job as a replacement.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you left Kailis in April of 1944 and went to the Kaunas Ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --where you stayed for a few months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone in your family work at all in the Kaunas Ghetto? Or you were just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --quartered there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were just-- we were just there, languishing. We got a little room where to sleep over for a few months. And then, when the news spread that they're going to liquidate the Kaunas Ghetto, it was-- a panic had stricken all the people. People were trying to find-- they called it [NON-ENGLISH], which was a hiding places to hide. There were a lot of people who went into hiding. And my father and-- has discovered a [INAUDIBLE] hiding place, where there were a lot of people going into. And he took us all in there. But before anything happened, we had to get out of there because there was so little oxygen left, my father couldn't breathe. He was dying right then and there. So we just walked out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in one of those buildings underground. They had a-- a place there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it just you and your family there in that hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was-- it-- there must've been maybe 50 or 60 people in that one spot. That's why there wasn't enough to-- to breathe over there. But they found that hiding place anyway later. And most of them were found out [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you walked out of the hiding place, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were told to march. There was the-- there was the train. They had a column of people. They had some trucks, and some walked towards the train. And they loaded us all onto that train. And they said-- they even said to us that they're taking us to a special labor camp. There's gonna be lots of food there. We're gonna be working there. And they even made up stories-- we're gonna be working on-- on ammunition there, and-- and gave us some details. This was their way to cheat, and to lie, and to make you believe something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the date that that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was-- I believe it was August of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they take you from then?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Now, we were all loaded up onto the trains. It was boxcars. And they closed the doors and put the-- the latch, the hook over. And the-- the train started rolling. And through the night, and day, and night, stopping and going, stopping and going. At one point, the train stropped. It was in the early morning. And the Germans opened the doors. And they told us, everybody stay in. We're going to go now to the camp. However, the women-- and there were some younger children there yet left-- must go out first, and then the men will follow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So out of the entire length of the train, all the women and all-- some of the children that were still remained, that still lived, were taken off. And as soon as the women got off the train, the Lithuanians and the-- and-- and the Germans ran by all the cars and they shut the doors. And the train took off.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the train took off-- that was in Stutthof, town of Stutthof, where my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stutthof is in Prussia, I believe. And that's where my mother, my sister, my aunt, and my-- my cousin Debbie, they were taken off there. And my father and I, and most of the Lithuanian people from Kaunas-- men-- were were taken into Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say Lithuanian people, you mean other Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were all mostly people from Kaunas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. How do you spell Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-U-T-F-- I believe F-F-H-O-F, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: T-T--

speaker: interviewee
text: H-O-F, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so that's where you were separated from your mother and your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where I was separated from my mother and my sister, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you and your father go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were brought into camp number one, which was a satellite of the Dachau concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-A-C-H-A-U.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you arrived in Dachau in, I guess, August 1944? When you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, exactly. In August 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you and your father do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was-- when we arrived in Dachau, the first day that we arrived, they put us into a roll call. Everybody had to stand at attention. And they counted us. And they gave us numbers. My number was 80984.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that 8-0-9-8-4?

speaker: interviewee
text: 8-0-9-8-4. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's right. That was my number. My father's number was 81478. And these numbers were not put on the-- tattooed on the arms. They were given us on the-- on the little white piece of cloth. And the number was written. It was-- and they gave us the striped garbs to wear, prisoner garbs. And I recall this terrible indignity. They told us to give up all our jewels, and all our monies, and everything. We had to give up all our clothes. Everything has to be given up. And some of the people have thrown their-- their possessions into the latrine, into the bathroom over there. There was an outhouse, like-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they felt that they didn't want to give it to the Germans. They just did it. They just threw it in there. So the Germans discovered that. And they selected a group of people, who-- made them undress completely. And they had to go in into the latrine, up to their necks in this-- and fish out all the jewels and everything that was put in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they pick the people to go into the latrine?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the-- we were all on a roll call, standing in the field. And the Nazis passed by-- you, you, you, you. As a matter of fact, they picked on one elderly man. And he was with his son. His son was next to him too. So the son begged that he should-- he wants to go instead of his father. So what they did instead, they made him join his father. And they went both to this filthy job. And then they were starting to assign to these huts. They were actually in the ground, cut out a long path. And we didn't have any beds or anything. It was just we laid on-- on the-- actually, on the ground. They had some straw spread out there. And that we were assigned-- each one was assigned to a separate-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they called it a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't how to express what I saw, but they called it a barrack. That's what it was. A barrack. And a few days later, some people have committed some minor thing. I don't know exactly what it was. But a roll call was called. And they put up a gallow. And I remember five people were hanged in front of everybody. We all had to stand and watch. There were also times where certain people that-- they put us-- by the way, they put us to work for building underground ammunition factories. The firms that were involved in building it were-- one was called the Holzmann company, and the other one's called the Moll company, M-O-L-L company. They were big builders. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape four of the interview of Jack Arnel.
